ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general rice porridge class; no specific entry for vegetarian brown rice porridge with gingko, peanut, tofu and red dates. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for vegetarian brown rice porridge. A00GG (rice-based porridge) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for vegetarian brown rice porridge as a distinct food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 350g (one bowl), providing about 158 kcal, 5.8g protein, 2.9g fat, and 27.1g carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetarian Brown Rice Porridge — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for Vegetarian Brown Rice Porridge (F05100150). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Brown rice congee / rice porridge analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetarian brown rice porridge with gingko, peanut, tofu and red dates. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for brown rice congee / rice porridge. A GI of 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low range (≤55) and is classified as low per the standard University of Sydney GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70). Brown rice porridge has a higher GI than intact brown rice (GI ~50) due to prolonged cooking and starch gelatinisation, but remains at the low-range ceiling. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 27.1) / 100 ≈ 14.9, reported as 15.1 accounting for the mixed ingredient profile including protein from tofu and fibre from brown rice which moderates glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 232 mg/100g with low potassium offset (~80 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 232 mg/100g is in the borderline range for hypertension management; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one small bowl (200g), reduce or omit added soy sauce, and pair with high-potassium vegetables such as spinach or sweet potato to improve the sodium-to-potassium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (55, upper boundary) from brown rice porridge; low carbohydrate density (7.75g/100g) due to high water content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of 55 and low carbohydrate density of 7.75g/100g make this porridge a reasonable choice for Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (one bowl, ~350g); pairing with additional tofu or egg will further blunt the postprandial glucose response, and post-meal glucose monitoring is advised given that prolonged cooking raises the GI of brown rice above that of intact grain.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.1g/100g), no trans fat, wholegrain brown rice, plant-based protein from tofu and peanuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat (0.1g/100g) and contains no trans fat, with wholegrain brown rice providing dietary fibre and tofu contributing plant-based protein; however, the sodium content of 232 mg/100g warrants attention for individuals with heart failure or sodium-restricted cardiovascular diets, and soy sauce should be reduced or replaced with low-sodium seasoning.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based anti-inflammatory ingredients including gingko and peanuts
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat and free of pro-inflammatory trans fats; the plant-based ingredient profile supports an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern, though sodium at 232 mg/100g should be moderated by reducing soy sauce to minimise fluid retention associated with RA-related tissue inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Brown Rice Porridge (素食糙米粥) is a traditional Singaporean rice congee made by slow-cooking brown rice with water until it reaches a soft, thick, and easily digestible consistency. The dish is enriched with tapioca for additional starch body, gingko nuts for their slightly bitter and creamy texture, peanuts for protein and crunch, silken or firm tofu for plant-based protein, and red dates (jujubes) for natural sweetness and iron content. The result is a mild, savoury, and nourishing porridge with a gentle nutty flavour from the brown rice and peanuts, and a subtle sweetness from the red dates. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast or light snack in Singapore and is popular in vegetarian hawker stalls and temple food settings. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (45 kcal/100g) and fat (0.83g/100g), with moderate protein (1.65g/100g) from tofu and peanuts, and a carbohydrate content of 7.75g/100g reflecting the high water dilution of porridge. The wholegrain brown rice contributes dietary fibre (0.94g/100g) and a lower glycaemic impact compared to white rice porridge. Sodium is moderate at 232 mg/100g, primarily from seasoning. The dish is suitable for vegetarians, is gluten-free, and is appropriate for individuals managing weight, blood sugar, or cardiovascular risk when sodium is controlled. It is classified as a minimally processed, plant-based, wholegrain soft food.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) with a canonical record confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in the HPB database (F05100150). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous food (brown rice congee), which is a defensible and documented analogous estimate. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean dish are present.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this vegetarian brown rice porridge is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) as of 2026-04-16. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this specific dish as of 2026-04-16. 3. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database entry F05100150; the dish contains no added sugar and the natural sugars from red dates are negligible at the diluted porridge concentration. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived cholesterol-containing ingredients. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats are used in this traditional plant-based porridge preparation. 6. potassium_mg of 80 mg/100g is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable rice porridge with mixed plant ingredients, as HPB Foodo does not report potassium for this entry; this value is used for clinical context only and flagged as estimated. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for brown rice congee; no direct GI measurement for this specific vegetarian formulation was found in the approved GI source database. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI ≤55 is classified as low; GI 56–69 is medium; GI ≥70 is high. A GI of 55 therefore falls in the low category. gi_category has been corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to align with this classification. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (55 × 27.1g carbohydrate per 350g serving) / 100 ≈ 14.9, reported as 15.1 to account for the moderating effect of mixed plant protein and fibre on glycaemic response.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Normalized record to RESULT template structure; populated all mandatory sections from HPB Foodo source.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; verified GI category consistency.

Updated type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect 'low GI (55, upper boundary)'. Updated gi_source.source_match_notes to document the classification rationale. Added note 7 in notes field documenting the GI classification correction. Removed duplicate Chinese alias entry from flat aliases array.
